_meta.note: Wylie is authoritative (ASCII, deterministic). The `tibetan` field was best-effort from handover.md §9 and has been verified: every string was (a) decoded code-point-by-code-point and checked against a hand-derived EWTS (Extended Wylie) encoding, and (b) cross-checked against authoritative external sources (Wikipedia, Wiktionary bo, Rangjung Yeshe Wiki / Tsadra, rigpawiki, studybuddhism). All 25 substantive strings matched with ZERO corrections; the two script marks (tsheg/shad) are definitional single code points. `tibetanSource` records the corroborating URL per term.
_meta.expansion: 2026-05-28: extended beyond the original Tibetan-script terms with beginner-facing concept terms (categories foundation/concept/practice/institution). New optional fields: `sanskrit` (IAST) and `primary` ('tibetan'|'sanskrit'|'english') telling the UI which form is the card headword; entries missing them default to primary='tibetan'. Entries with a `wylie` but an empty `tibetan` and tibetanVerified:false still need their Tibetan Unicode sourced + verified (THL / Wiktionary-bo / Rangjung-Yeshe) before shipping — do NOT guess the glyphs. Sanskrit/English-primary terms carry no Tibetan headword (tibetan empty, tibetanVerified:true, source '—').

terms.gloss: "oral / whispered transmission" school

terms.tibetanSource: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karma_Kagyu (karma = ཀརྨ་ per 2nd Karmapa; bka' brgyud per Wiktionary)
terms.gloss: largest Kagyü sub-lineage, headed by the Karmapa
head of Karma Kagyü; the "Black Crown Lama"
qualified spiritual teacher (= guru)
"precious one"; honorific for high tulkus

A teacher recognized as the conscious rebirth of a previous master, returning to continue their work. The Karmapa line was the first such recognized reincarnation lineage; the Dalai Lamas later followed the same model.
"diamond/thunderbolt"; root of Vajrayāna

terms.keywords: vajradhara, primordial buddha, source of the lineage
terms.gloss: primordial buddha; source of the transmission
The four foundational/preliminary practices: *refuge & prostrations*, *Vajrasattva* purification, *mandala offerings*, and *guru yoga*.

"Going for refuge" — formally placing one's trust in the Three Jewels (*Buddha*, *Dharma*, *Sangha*) and the lineage. It marks entry onto the Buddhist path and, with prostrations, is the first of the four Ngöndro foundations.

terms.keywords: transference of consciousness, conscious dying, death meditation
terms.gloss: "transference"; meditation of conscious dying

terms.tibetanSource: https://www.rigpawiki.org/index.php?title=Mahamudra (phyag chen, short form of phyag rgya chen po)
terms.gloss: "Great Seal" — the highest meditation of the Kagyü lineage, a direct recognition of the mind's own nature as the inseparable union of openness (emptiness) and clear awareness, beyond concepts. It is the goal toward which the other practices build.

terms.keywords: inner heat, mystic heat, six yogas of naropa
terms.gloss: "Inner heat" — an advanced yoga that works with the subtle body's energy channels to generate a blissful warmth, used as a basis for deeper realization. One of the Six Yogas of Nāropa.
Dream yoga — training to become lucid and to practice consciously within the dream state, recognizing its illusory nature. One of the Six Yogas of Nāropa.
Clear-light yoga — recognizing the luminous, knowing nature of mind, especially in deep sleep and at the moment of death. One of the Six Yogas of Nāropa.
Illusory-body yoga — recognizing that the body and all appearances are dream-like and insubstantial, like a reflection. One of the Six Yogas of Nāropa.
intermediate state (esp. between death & rebirth)

A meditational buddha-form embodying an enlightened quality — for example Chenrezig for compassion. The practitioner visualizes and identifies with the yidam not as an external god but as a reflection of their own enlightened nature.
An empowerment or initiation given by a qualified teacher that authorizes and connects a student to a particular tantric practice — the formal doorway into working with a specific yidam.

terms.keywords: avalokiteshvara, compassion, om mani peme hung
terms.gloss: The buddha of compassion (Sanskrit Avalokiteśvara), one of the most beloved meditational forms in Tibet; his mantra is the well-known om mani peme hung. The Dalai Lamas are traditionally regarded as his emanations.
post-death meditative state of a realized master
the "Red Crown" regent lama
the Buddha's teaching; phenomena/reality

The spiritual community — the third of the Three Jewels. Traditionally the assembly of practitioners and realized beings; in Diamond Way, the worldwide network of lay friends who practice and support one another.

terms.tibetanSource: Unicode U+0F0B TIBETAN MARK INTERSYLLABIC TSHEG (definitional)
terms.gloss: the syllable-separating dot
the phrase/clause terminator

terms.tibetanSource: https://kagyuoffice.org/kagyu-lineage/the-golden-rosary/ (bka' brgyud gser phreng, Golden Garland of the Kagyu Lineage)
terms.gloss: "golden rosary" — the lineage succession (site title)

terms.tibetanSource: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Refuge_(Buddhism) (དཀོན་མཆོག་གསུམ, Wylie dkon mchog gsum); components དཀོན་མཆོག + གསུམ per Wiktionary(bo)
terms.keywords: refuge, triple gem, triratna, buddha dharma sangha
terms.gloss: The three objects of refuge that mark entry onto the Buddhist path: the *Buddha* (the awakened example), the *Dharma* (his teaching), and the *Sangha* (the community of practitioners).

terms.phonetic: Four Thoughts That Turn the Mind
terms.tibetanSource: https://www.rigpawiki.org/index.php?title=Four_thoughts (བློ་ལྡོག་རྣམ་བཞི, Wylie blo ldog rnam bzhi); cross-checked rywiki blo_ldog_rnam_bzhi
terms.keywords: turning the mind, four reminders, common preliminaries, four ordinary foundations
terms.gloss: Four reflections that turn the mind toward practice: the *rarity* of a precious human life, *impermanence* and death, *karma* (cause and effect), and the *unsatisfactoriness* of cyclic existence. Also called the four common preliminaries.
Action and its results — the principle that intentional deeds leave impressions that shape later experience. In Diamond Way's idiom, 'good and bad impressions,' not fate.
The round of conditioned existence — the cycle of birth, death and rebirth driven by ignorance and disturbing emotions, contrasted with liberation.
The 'extinguishing' of ignorance and suffering — liberation from cyclic existence. In Mahayana, not a place but the mind freed of its obscurations.
The truth that all compounded things — bodies, relationships, moods, worlds — are in constant change and will end; one of the Four Thoughts.
The beneficial potential built up through generosity, ethics and practice — in Diamond Way usually phrased as 'positive impressions' that ripen as good circumstances and progress on the path.

terms.tibetanSource: rywiki.tsadra.org/index.php/tshogs_gnyis; Encyclopedia of Buddhism (tshogs gnyis, 'tsok nyi')
terms.keywords: merit and wisdom, two stores, tsok nyi
terms.gloss: The two stores gathered as the basis for awakening: *merit* (positive impressions, from compassionate activity) and *wisdom* (insight into the nature of mind and reality).
The rare opportunity of a human life with the freedom and conditions to practice the Dharma; the first of the Four Thoughts, meant to inspire one not to waste it.
Not nothingness, but the absence of independent, fixed existence in things and in the self — they arise dependently. Realizing it directly is the heart of wisdom.

terms.tibetanSource: https://www.rigpawiki.org/index.php?title=Nature_of_mind (སེམས་ཉིད་, Wylie sems nyid); components སེམས + ཉིད per Wiktionary(bo)/Tsadra
terms.gloss: Mind's own essence as the inseparable union of openness (emptiness) and knowing clarity, beyond concept — what Mahamudra points out directly.

terms.tibetanSource: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddha-nature (bde gshegs snying po / sugatagarbha; cf. de bzhin gshegs pa'i snying po)
terms.gloss: The innate potential for awakening present in every being — the 'seed' that practice uncovers rather than manufactures.
The 'awakening mind' — the wish to reach enlightenment for the benefit of all beings. The motivational heart of Mahayana and Vajrayana.
One who commits to attaining buddhahood for the sake of all beings, rather than seeking personal liberation alone.

terms.keywords: relative and ultimate, conventional and absolute
terms.gloss: Reality described on two levels at once: the *relative* truth of everyday appearances, and the *ultimate* truth of their empty, dependent nature.

terms.keywords: diamond way, diamond vehicle, tantric buddhism, mantrayana
terms.gloss: The tantric 'diamond / thunderbolt vehicle' of Buddhism as a whole — its plain English name is 'the diamond way' (lowercase), which is why it is so easily confused with the Diamond Way organization.
Both the texts and the methods of Vajrayana — working directly with energy, devotion and identification with enlightenment rather than renunciation alone.

terms.tibetanSource: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sukhavati (བདེ་བ་ཅན་, Wylie bde ba can); cross-checked Wiktionary(bo) བདེ་བ་ཅན
terms.gloss: A buddha-realm — here Amitābha's 'Realm of Great Bliss' (Dewachen / Sukhāvatī) — into which consciousness is transferred in Phowa.
The Buddha of Limitless Light, presiding over the pure land of Dewachen; the destination of consciousness in Phowa.

terms.keywords: trikaya, three bodies, dharmakaya sambhogakaya nirmanakaya
terms.gloss: Three 'bodies' or dimensions of a buddha: *dharmakaya* (the formless nature), *sambhogakaya* (radiant form perceptible to realized beings), and *nirmanakaya* (manifest physical form, e.g. a teacher).

terms.tibetanSource: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samatha (ཞི་གནས་, Wylie zhi-gnas); cross-checked Wiktionary(bo) ཞི + གནས
terms.keywords: calm abiding, calming the mind, peaceful abiding, meditation, shinay
terms.gloss: Meditation that settles and stabilizes the mind — 'calm abiding' (Tib. shinay, zhi gnas) — typically by resting attention on the breath or an object; the foundation on which insight is built.

Meditation of clear seeing into the nature of mind and phenomena (Tib. lhaktong, lhag mthong); the insight that completes calm abiding and aims at the direct experience of mind's nature — enlightenment.
Holding a buddha-form, light or pure realm in the mind's eye — the core method of deity practice, used to identify with enlightened qualities rather than the ordinary self-image.
A symbolic representation of an enlightened realm or of the whole universe; in Ngöndro, repeatedly 'offering the mandala' cultivates generosity and gathers merit.

terms.keywords: dorje sempa, purification, hundred-syllable mantra, 100-syllable mantra
terms.gloss: The buddha of purification (Dorje Sempa); meditating on him with the hundred-syllable mantra cleanses harmful impressions. The second of the four Ngöndro foundations.
Sacred syllables recited in practice to protect and transform the mind (for example Chenrezig's om mani peme hung).

terms.tibetanSource: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guru_yoga (Wylie bla ma'i rnal 'byor); composed from Wiktionary(bo) བླ་མ + genitive འི + རྣལ་འབྱོར
terms.gloss: Uniting one's mind with the enlightened mind of the teacher and lineage; in Diamond Way this centers on the meditation on the 16th Karmapa, and it is the fourth Ngöndro practice.

terms.tibetanSource: https://www.rigpawiki.org/index.php?title=Prostration (ཕྱག་འཚལ, Wylie phyag 'tshal); component ཕྱག per Wiktionary(bo), cross-checked rywiki phyag_'tshal
terms.gloss: Full-body bows combined with taking refuge; the first Ngöndro practice, working with pride and joining body, speech and mind.

terms.tibetanSource: https://www.rigpawiki.org/index.php?title=Three_Roots (རྩ་བ་གསུམ, Wylie rtsa ba gsum); components རྩ་བ + གསུམ per Wiktionary(bo), cross-checked rywiki rtsa_ba_gsum
terms.gloss: The Vajrayana sources of accomplishment taken alongside the Three Jewels: the *lama* (root of blessing), the *yidam* (root of accomplishment), and the *protectors*/dakinis (root of activity).

terms.tibetanSource: — (Sanskrit term; Tib. grub thob chen po)
terms.gloss: A 'great accomplished one' — the often unconventional Indian tantric adepts, such as Tilopa and Nāropa, at the head of the lineage.

Diamond Way's defining daily guru yoga: visualizing the 16th Karmapa in his Black Crown form, receiving the lights of body, speech and mind, then resting — a gateway to Mahamudra that binds the global lay sangha.

The traditional intensive Kagyu training (about three years and three months) in which committed practitioners complete the advanced yogas; part of the lineage's monastic inheritance rather than everyday lay practice.

The Karmapa's emblem: an immaterial 'wisdom crown' of dakini-hair perceptible only to the realized, and a physical replica gifted (by tradition) by the Ming Yongle Emperor. Its ceremonial display was a signature rite of the 16th Karmapa's Western tours.

In this tradition a 'yoga' (Tib. naljor, 'union') is a discipline of meditation that joins the practitioner with enlightened reality — not a system of physical postures. The Six Yogas of Nāropa are advanced practices of this kind.
Tilopa (988–1069), the Indian mahāsiddha at the head of the Kagyü lineage, said to have received its teachings directly from the buddha Vajradhara; Nāropa's teacher.

Nāropa (1016–1100), an Indian mahāsiddha and abbot of Nālandā who left scholarship for yogic practice; Tilopa's chief disciple, the systematizer of the Six Yogas that bear his name, and Marpa's teacher.
Marpa Lotsāwa (1012–1097), 'the Translator' — a Tibetan layman who made arduous journeys to India and carried the Kagyü tantric transmissions back to Tibet; Nāropa's student and Milarepa's teacher.

Milarepa (1052–1135), Tibet's beloved yogi-poet who, after a dark early life, reached enlightenment in a single lifetime through intense practice under Marpa; his 'Hundred Thousand Songs' are foundational.
Gampopa (1079–1153), Milarepa's chief heir — a former physician and monk who fused yogic Mahāmudrā with the monastic gradual path, becoming the institutional founder of the Kagyü school.
Düsum Khyenpa (1110–1193), a disciple of Gampopa who founded the Karma Kagyü and was recognized, retrospectively, as the 1st Karmapa; he established Tsurphu monastery.
